original_hypothesis.title: Tokenomics.checkpoint lacks reentrancy guard around external treasury.rebalanceTreasury call, enabling epoch state corruption if treasury is malicious/compromised
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Tokenomics.checkpoint() performs an external call to ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) before updating epochCounter, without using the existing _locked reentrancy guard variable. A malicious or compromised Treasury could re-enter checkpoint() (or other Tokenomics functions) mid-execution to attempt double-settlement, inconsistent effectiveBond/maxBond updates, or epoch point overwrites.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even though Treasury is a trusted boundary, this is a concrete code defect: a reentrancy lock exists but is not applied in the most stateful function. If trust is violated (compromised key/upgrade), impact is large because it can violate epoch monotonicity and accounting invariants.

proof_of_signal.lines: checkpoint(): external call ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(...) occurs while checkpoint state updates are in-progress; _locked is never checked/updated
proof_of_signal.pattern: external call in state-transition function without reentrancy guard

preconditions: Treasury contract can execute a callback into Tokenomics during rebalanceTreasury
Callback reaches checkpoint() again (and passes timing checks) or mutates related state in a way that breaks accounting

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: no reentrancy guard despite existing _locked field
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no nonReentrant check and no state 'entered' flag

analysis_questions: Can a re-entrant checkpoint() pass the same diffNumSeconds checks in the same transaction?
Are there other Tokenomics mutators callable by Treasury that could corrupt accounting mid-checkpoint?
Does Treasury.rebalanceTreasury ever call back into Tokenomics in the real implementation?
validation_requirements: Inspect Treasury implementation for callbacks to Tokenomics
Write a malicious Treasury PoC that re-enters checkpoint and observe state
Add invariant tests around epochCounter monotonicity under reentrancy

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Treasury is malicious, it calls back into Tokenomics.checkpoint during rebalanceTreasury to force a second partial settlement, causing epochCounter or effectiveBond to deviate from intended single-step transition.
prerequisites: Malicious or compromised Treasury (upgrade or key compromise)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Mis-accounting of inflation/bonds and incorrect incentive allocation across epochs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users reliant on epoch settlement correctness
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require governance migration depending on severity of state corruption

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing adversarial treasury callback tests for Tokenomics.checkpoint.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor unusual multiple Tokenomics events in one tx (EpochSettled/IDFUpdated)

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
